pathways.label: Adult conviction sealing or expungement under Ohio Rev. Code § 2953.32
pathways.summary: An eligible offender may apply to the sentencing court for an Ohio conviction, or to a court of common pleas for an out-of-state or federal conviction, unless the conviction is excluded by 2953.32(A). The court must check timing, pending cases, rehabilitation, prosecutor/victim objections, and whether the persons interest in relief outweighs legitimate government needs. ("https://codes.ohio.gov/ohio-revised-code/section-2953.32") Sealing - 1 or 2 F3 convictions 3 years after final discharge Sealing - F4, F5, or misdemeanor 1 year after final discharge Sealing - soliciting improper compensation under 2921.43 7 years after final discharge…

Wilma should flag likely ineligible or manual review if the conviction is:
most traffic/motor-vehicle chapters: Chapters 4506, 4507, 4510, 4511, or 4549, or substantially similar municipal ordinances;
felony offense of violence that is not a sexually oriented offense;
sexually oriented offense while subject to Chapter 2950 registration;
offense where the victim was under 13, except nonsupport under 2919.21;
M1 or M2 domestic violence under 2919.25 or similar municipal ordinance;
F3 where the person has too many other convictions under the statutes F3 counting rule. ("https://codes.ohio.gov/ohio-revised-code/section-2953.32")

Some offenses are sealable but not expungeable, including M3/M4 domestic violence under 2919.25 and protection-order violations under 2919.27 or substantially similar municipal ordinances. ("https://codes.ohio.gov/ohio-revised-code/section-2953.32")

Ohio has special counting rules. Convictions connected with the same act or committed at the same time can count as one, and two or three convictions from the same indictment, plea, or official proceeding involving related acts within three months may count as one unless the court finds that is not in the public interest. ("https://codes.ohio.gov/ohio-revised-code/section-2953.32")
Wilma rule: Never count cases by memory. Ask for the full docket list and disposition dates.

The hearing must be held 45-90 days after filing; the prosecutor may object; the court weighs the persons interest against legitimate government needs; if granted, the records are expunged and the person/court may reply that no record exists. The filing fee is $50 unless the applicant is indigent. ("https://codes.ohio.gov/ohio-revised-code/section-2953.321")

means certain Chapter 2925 violations that are M4 or minor misdemeanors, or substantially equivalent municipal ordinances. The prosecutor can file after the corresponding 2953.32 waiting period expires, and the subject person and victim may object. ("https://codes.ohio.gov/ohio-revised-code/section-2953.39")

For juvenile expungement, 2151.358 requires the juvenile court to expunge records sealed under 2151.356 five years after the sealing order or upon the persons 23rd birthday, whichever comes earlier. A person with a sealed juvenile record may also apply earlier, and the court may expunge if rehabilitation is shown. ("https://codes.ohio.gov/ohio-revised-code/section-2151.358")

The evidentiary standard depends on the route: preponderance of the evidence for the prostitution-related category and clear and convincing evidence for the broader misdemeanor/F4/F5 category. The statute excludes expungement for aggravated murder, murder, and rape convictions under 2903.01, 2903.02, and 2907.02. If granted, the record cannot be used for a criminal-record check, and the applicant/court may reply that no record exists. ("https://codes.ohio.gov/ohio-revised-code/section-2953.36")

Juvenile records use separate law. Under 2151.356, records for delinquency adjudications involving aggravated murder, murder, or rape cannot be sealed. The juvenile court must promptly seal records in several circumstances, including no complaint filed after referral, successful diversion for certain underage alcohol charges, and some dismissals or not-delinquent findings. For other eligible juvenile records, the court can seal on its own motion or on application once timing requirements are met, and there is no filing fee. ("https://codes.ohio.gov/ohio-revised-code/section-2151.356")

pathways.label: Adult non-conviction sealing or expungement under § 2953.33
pathways.summary: Ohios non-conviction route covers not guilty findings, dismissals, grand jury no bills, and pardons. Not guilty Any time after entry Dismissal Any time after dismissal entry, subject to limitations and multiple-charge rules No bill Usually 2 years after grand jury reports the no bill Governor pardon Any time after absolute/partial pardon, or after pardon conditions are met
ruleClauses: Ohios non-conviction route covers not guilty findings, dismissals, grand jury no bills, and pardons.
Not guilty Any time after entry
Dismissal Any time after dismissal entry, subject to limitations and multiple-charge rules
No bill Usually 2 years after grand jury reports the no bill
Governor pardon Any time after absolute/partial pardon, or after pardon conditions are met

For dismissals, the court must determine whether the dismissal was with or without prejudice; if without prejudice, it must determine whether the statute of limitations has expired. The court also checks pending proceedings, prosecutor objections, and whether the persons interest outweighs legitimate government needs. (
"https://codes.ohio.gov/ohio-revised-code/section-2953.33")
Wilma rule: A dismissal is not always simple. If it was dismissed without prejudice and the statute of limitations has not expired, do not tell the user the record is ready.

Multiple charges from the same act - 2953.61 Ohio has a trap rule for mixed-disposition cases. If a person was charged with two or more offenses from the same act and at least one charge has a different final disposition than the others, they generally cannot seal or expunge only part of the record until they can seal/expunge all related charges. There is a limited exception for certain traffic convictions, but the court cannot order only a portion of those records sealed or expunged. ("https://codes.ohio.gov/ohio-revised-code/section-2953.61") Wilma rule: If one incident produced multiple charges, ask for every charge and every disposition before routing.

pathways.label: Marijuana / hashish possession expungement under § 2953.321
pathways.summary: Ohio has a specific marijuana/hashish possession expungement route effective March 20, 2026. It applies to certain dismissed, convicted, or guilty-plea records involving specified marijuana or hashish possession offenses under 2925.11, including hashish possession of not more than 15 grams for certain divisions. The person applies to the sentencing court, and the application must identify the applicant, offense, date, court, evidence of eligibility, and request expungement. ("https://codes.ohio.gov/ohio-revised-code/section-2953.321") The hearing must be held 45-90 days after filing; the prosecutor may object; the court weighs the persons…

pathways.label: Human-trafficking-survivor conviction expungement under § 2953.36
pathways.summary: Ohio allows expungement of certain convictions where the persons participation in the offense resulted from being a victim of human trafficking. It covers people convicted of prostitution-related offenses under 2907.24, 2907.241, or 2907.25, and also people convicted of a misdemeanor or F4/F5 whose participation resulted from trafficking victimization. The application may be filed at any time and can request relief for more than one offense, though the court considers each offense separately. ("https://codes.ohio.gov/ohio-revised-code/section-2953.36") The evidentiary standard depends on the route: preponderance of the evidence for the…

exclusionRules.ruleText: An eligible offender may apply to the sentencing court for an Ohio conviction, or to a court of common pleas for an out-of-state or federal conviction, unless the conviction is excluded by 2953.32(A). The court must check timing, pending cases, rehabilitation, prosecutor/victim objections, and whether the persons interest in relief outweighs legitimate government needs. ("https://codes.ohio.gov/ohio-revised-code/section-2953.32")
Wilma should flag likely ineligible or manual review if the conviction is:

The evidentiary standard depends on the route: preponderance of the evidence for the prostitution-related category and clear and convincing evidence for the broader misdemeanor/F4/F5 category. The statute excludes expungement for aggravated murder, murder, and rape convictions under 2903.01, 2903.02, and 2907.02. If granted, the record cannot be used for a criminal-record check, and the applicant/court may reply that no record exists. ("https://codes.ohio.gov/ohio-revised-code/section-2953.36")

Juvenile records use separate law. Under 2151.356, records for delinquency adjudications involving aggravated murder, murder, or rape cannot be sealed. The juvenile court must promptly seal records in several circumstances, including no complaint filed after referral, successful diversion for certain underage alcohol charges, and some dismissals or not-delinquent findings. For other eligible juvenile records, the court can seal on its own motion or on application once timing requirements are met, and there is no filing fee. ("https://codes.ohio.gov/ohio-revised-code/section-2151.356")

sourceSections.title: Remedy definitions Wilma must understand
sourceSections.text:
Sealing
Sealing makes the record private from most employers, landlords, schools, and the general public, but it does not erase or delete the record. Ohio Legal Help states that a sealed record still exists and may still be seen by certain employers, licensing boards, prosecutors, judges, and police. (

"https://www.ohiolegalhelp.org/topic/seal-criminal-record")
Expungement
Ohio law treats expungement as stronger than sealing. Under 2953.31,
expunge
for many Ohio expungement sections means to destroy, delete, and erase a record so it is permanently irretrievable. For 2953.32 conviction expungements, BCI keeps a limited record for law-enforcement employment qualification/disqualification, while other entities receiving notice must destroy, delete, and erase the record. ("https://codes.ohio.gov/ohio-revised-code/section-2953.31")
Wilma rule: Do not say
completely gone from everywhere.
Say:
Expungement is stronger than sealing and generally requires agencies to destroy/delete/erase the record, but Ohio law still contains specific exceptions, including limited BCI retention for some conviction expungements.

sourceSections.title: Adult conviction sealing / expungement - 2953.32
sourceSections.text:
Basic eligibility route
An eligible offender may apply to the sentencing court for an Ohio conviction, or to a court of common pleas for an out-of-state or federal conviction, unless the conviction is excluded by 2953.32(A). The court must check timing, pending cases, rehabilitation, prosecutor/victim objections, and whether the persons interest in relief outweighs legitimate government needs. ("https://codes.ohio.gov/ohio-revised-code/section-2953.32")
Waiting periods - convictions

Route Waiting period
Sealing - 1 or 2 F3 convictions 3 years after final discharge
Sealing - F4, F5, or misdemeanor 1 year after final discharge
Sealing - soliciting improper compensation under 2921.43 7 years after final discharge
Sealing - person formerly subject to Chapter 2950 registration 5 years after registration requirements end or terminate
Sealing - minor misdemeanor 6 months after final discharge
Expungement - misdemeanor 1 year after final discharge
Expungement - minor misdemeanor 6 months after final discharge
Expungement - felony 10 years after the date the person first becomes eligible to apply for sealing for that felony
Misdemeanor bail forfeiture - sealing Any time after bail forfeiture entry
Misdemeanor bail forfeiture - expungement Generally 1 year after entry; 6 months for minor misdemeanor
These timing rules come from 2953.32(B). (

"https://codes.ohio.gov/ohio-revised-code/section-2953.32")
Major conviction disqualifiers
Wilma should flag likely ineligible or manual review if the conviction is:
most traffic/motor-vehicle chapters: Chapters 4506, 4507, 4510, 4511, or 4549, or substantially similar municipal ordinances;
felony offense of violence that is not a sexually oriented offense;
sexually oriented offense while subject to Chapter 2950 registration;
offense where the victim was under 13, except nonsupport under 2919.21;
theft in office under 2921.41;
F1 or F2;
M1 or M2 domestic violence under 2919.25 or similar municipal ordinance;
F3 where the person has too many other convictions under the statutes F3 counting rule. ("https://codes.ohio.gov/ohio-revised-code/section-2953.32")
Some offenses are sealable but not expungeable, including M3/M4 domestic violence under 2919.25 and protection-order violations under 2919.27 or substantially similar municipal ordinances. ("https://codes.ohio.gov/ohio-revised-code/section-2953.32")
Counting convictions
Ohio has special counting rules. Convictions connected with the same act or committed at the same time can count as one, and two or three convictions from the same indictment, plea, or official proceeding involving related acts within three months may count as one unless the court finds that is not in the public interest. ("https://codes.ohio.gov/ohio-revised-code/section-2953.32")
Wilma rule: Never count cases by memory. Ask for the full docket list and disposition dates.

sourceSections.title: Non-conviction sealing / expungement - 2953.33
sourceSections.text:
Ohios non-conviction route covers not guilty findings, dismissals, grand jury no bills, and pardons.

Outcome Timing
Not guilty Any time after entry
Dismissal Any time after dismissal entry, subject to limitations and multiple-charge rules
No bill Usually 2 years after grand jury reports the no bill
Governor pardon Any time after absolute/partial pardon, or after pardon conditions are met
For dismissals, the court must determine whether the dismissal was with or without prejudice; if without prejudice, it must determine whether the statute of limitations has expired. The court also checks pending proceedings, prosecutor objections, and whether the persons interest outweighs legitimate government needs. (

"https://codes.ohio.gov/ohio-revised-code/section-2953.33")
Wilma rule: A dismissal is not always simple. If it was dismissed without prejudice and the statute of limitations has not expired, do not tell the user the record is ready.
6. Multiple charges from the same act - 2953.61
Ohio has a trap rule for mixed-disposition cases. If a person was charged with two or more offenses from the same act and at least one charge has a different final disposition than the others, they generally cannot seal or expunge only part of the record until they can seal/expunge all related charges. There is a limited exception for certain traffic convictions, but the court cannot order only a portion of those records sealed or expunged. ("https://codes.ohio.gov/ohio-revised-code/section-2953.61")
Wilma rule: If one incident produced multiple charges, ask for every charge and every disposition before routing.

sourceSections.title: Marijuana / hashish possession expungement - 2953.321
sourceSections.text:
Ohio has a specific marijuana/hashish possession expungement route effective March 20, 2026. It applies to certain dismissed, convicted, or guilty-plea records involving specified marijuana or hashish possession offenses under 2925.11, including hashish possession of not more than 15 grams for certain divisions. The person applies to the sentencing court, and the application must identify the applicant, offense, date, court, evidence of eligibility, and request expungement. ("https://codes.ohio.gov/ohio-revised-code/section-2953.321")
The hearing must be held 45-90 days after filing; the prosecutor may object; the court weighs the persons interest against legitimate government needs; if granted, the records are expunged and the person/court may reply that no record exists. The filing fee is $50 unless the applicant is indigent. ("https://codes.ohio.gov/ohio-revised-code/section-2953.321")
Wilma rule: Do not route every marijuana case here. Confirm the exact 2925.11 division, substance, amount, and whether the conviction/dismissal/plea occurred before the effective date of the section.

sourceSections.title: Human-trafficking survivor conviction expungement - 2953.36
sourceSections.text:
Ohio allows expungement of certain convictions where the persons participation in the offense resulted from being a victim of human trafficking. It covers people convicted of prostitution-related offenses under 2907.24, 2907.241, or 2907.25, and also people convicted of a misdemeanor or F4/F5 whose participation resulted from trafficking victimization. The application may be filed at any time and can request relief for more than one offense, though the court considers each offense separately. ("https://codes.ohio.gov/ohio-revised-code/section-2953.36")
The evidentiary standard depends on the route: preponderance of the evidence for the prostitution-related category and clear and convincing evidence for the broader misdemeanor/F4/F5 category. The statute excludes expungement for aggravated murder, murder, and rape convictions under 2903.01, 2903.02, and 2907.02. If granted, the record cannot be used for a criminal-record check, and the applicant/court may reply that no record exists. ("https://codes.ohio.gov/ohio-revised-code/section-2953.36")
Wilma trauma rule: Ask gently:
Was this charge connected to trafficking, coercion, exploitation, survival circumstances, or someone forcing or directing you to do something connected to the case?

sourceSections.title: Human-trafficking survivor non-conviction expungement - 2953.521
sourceSections.text: A person found not guilty or named in a dismissed complaint, indictment, or information may seek expungement if the case resulted from the person being a victim of human trafficking. The application may be filed any time after the not-guilty or dismissal entry. The court checks trafficking connection by preponderance of the evidence, dismissal prejudice/statute-of-limitations issues, pending criminal proceedings, prosecutor objections, and government-interest balancing. ("https://codes.ohio.gov/ohio-revised-code/section-2953.521")

sourceSections.title: Certain firearm/carry convictions - 2953.35
sourceSections.text:
Ohio has a narrow expungement route for certain older firearm/carry-related convictions, including specified former versions of 2923.16 and 2923.12. The application is filed in the sentencing court; the court checks whether the conduct would no longer violate the relevant provision, considers prosecutor objections, and weighs the applicants interest against government needs. The fee is $50 unless indigent. ("https://codes.ohio.gov/ohio-revised-code/section-2953.35")
Wilma rule: Do not treat this as a general firearm expungement. It is tied to specific old statutory versions and authorization language.
11. Prosecutor application for low-level controlled substance offenses - 2953.39
For a low-level controlled substance offense, the prosecutor may apply to the sentencing court to seal or expunge the record.
Low-level controlled substance offense
means certain Chapter 2925 violations that are M4 or minor misdemeanors, or substantially equivalent municipal ordinances. The prosecutor can file after the corresponding 2953.32 waiting period expires, and the subject person and victim may object. ("https://codes.ohio.gov/ohio-revised-code/section-2953.39")
Wilma rule: This is a prosecutor-initiated pathway, not the normal user-filed application. Flag it as
ask prosecutor / legal aid review.

sourceSections.title: Juvenile sealing and expungement
sourceSections.text:
Juvenile records use separate law. Under 2151.356, records for delinquency adjudications involving aggravated murder, murder, or rape cannot be sealed. The juvenile court must promptly seal records in several circumstances, including no complaint filed after referral, successful diversion for certain underage alcohol charges, and some dismissals or not-delinquent findings. For other eligible juvenile records, the court can seal on its own motion or on application once timing requirements are met, and there is no filing fee. ("https://codes.ohio.gov/ohio-revised-code/section-2151.356")
For juvenile expungement, 2151.358 requires the juvenile court to expunge records sealed under 2151.356 five years after the sealing order or upon the persons 23rd birthday, whichever comes earlier. A person with a sealed juvenile record may also apply earlier, and the court may expunge if rehabilitation is shown. ("https://codes.ohio.gov/ohio-revised-code/section-2151.358")

sourceSections.title: Filing instructions
sourceSections.text:
Adult conviction / bail forfeiture
File in the sentencing court for an Ohio conviction or in a court of common pleas for an out-of-state or federal conviction. The court schedules a hearing, notifies the prosecutor at least 60 days before the hearing, and holds the hearing 45-90 days after filing. The prosecutor may object at least 30 days before the hearing, and victims may be heard if applicable. ("https://codes.ohio.gov/ohio-revised-code/section-2953.32")
Non-conviction
File in the court where the case was pending, dismissed, resulted in not guilty, or where the grand jury no bill was reported. The court holds a hearing 45-90 days after filing, and the prosecutor may object. ("https://codes.ohio.gov/ohio-revised-code/section-2953.33")
After order is granted
For conviction/bail forfeiture sealing or expungement, the court forwards the order and fingerprint record to BCI, or orders fingerprinting if required. The Ohio Attorney General also instructs that signed sealing orders should be sent to BCI with the sealing/expungement request form and may be faxed or emailed to BCI. ("https://codes.ohio.gov/ohio-revised-code/section-2953.32")

sourceSections.title: Fees
sourceSections.text:
Route Fee
2953.32 conviction/bail forfeiture sealing or expungement $50 application fee unless indigent, plus possible local court fee up to $50
Dismissal / not guilty / no bill sealing Ohio Legal Help says no fee
2953.321 marijuana/hashish expungement $50 unless indigent
2953.35 firearm/carry expungement $50 unless indigent
2953.36 trafficking survivor conviction expungement $50 unless indigent
Juvenile sealing/expungement No filing fee under juvenile sealing statute
The current 2953.32 fee provision allows a $50 application fee and a possible local court fee of not more than $50; Ohio Legal Help says there is no fee for dismissals, not-guilty cases, or no-bill charges. (

"https://codes.ohio.gov/ohio-revised-code/section-2953.32")

sourceSections.title: Record request process
Wilma waiting-period table
sourceSections.text:
Case type Sealing wait Expungement wait
Minor misdemeanor conviction 6 months after final discharge 6 months after final discharge
Misdemeanor conviction 1 year after final discharge 1 year after final discharge
F4/F5 conviction 1 year after final discharge 10 years after sealing eligibility date
One or two F3 convictions 3 years after final discharge 10 years after sealing eligibility date
2921.43 soliciting improper compensation 7 years after final discharge Treat as felony expungement timing if felony; verify
Former Chapter 2950 registration case 5 years after registration ends/terminates Verify statute and offense
Not guilty / dismissal Any time after entry, subject to dismissal-without-prejudice limitations Same route under 2953.33 unless excluded
Grand jury no bill 2 years after no-bill report Same route under 2953.33 unless excluded
Misdemeanor bail forfeiture Any time after entry 1 year after entry, or 6 months for minor misdemeanor
Marijuana/hashish 2953.321 Specific expungement route Any time on/after March 20, 2026 if statutory criteria met
Human-trafficking survivor conviction Specific expungement route Any time
Juvenile sealed record Juvenile sealing first Expunged 5 years after sealing or by age 23, whichever earlier, unless earlier application granted

sourceSections.title: Wilma intake decision tree
Agent-facing summary
sourceSections.text:
For Ohio, train Wilma with this core logic:
Ohio has both sealing and expungement.Convictions route through 2953.32 unless a special statute applies.Dismissals/not-guilty/no-bill route through 2953.33.F1/F2, felony violence, active sex-offender registration, most traffic, theft in office, domestic violence, protection order, victim-under-13, and some F3 patterns are major danger zones.Marijuana/hashish now has a special 2026 expungement path.Human-trafficking survivor relief is broad and trauma-sensitive.Juvenile records are separate and often seal first, then expunge later.Do not promise relief until Wilma has the docket, exact statute, disposition, final discharge date, and pending-charge status.
